quote_text: Our FICC and equities businesses delivered their strongest first quarter in over a decade, driven by elevated client activity amid heightened macro volatility and ongoing portfolio repositioning across institutional accounts.

We are seeing a meaningful inflection in M&A advisory as boardrooms gain confidence. Our pipeline of AI-related mandates has tripled year-over-year, spanning semiconductor supply chain restructurings, data center infrastructure financing, and large-scale cloud platform partnerships.

Capital markets activity should continue to benefit from the normalization of the rate environment, though the recent tariff announcements have introduced some near-term uncertainty that could delay certain cross-border transactions.

Our asset and wealth management division gathered twenty-six billion in long-term fee-based net inflows during the quarter. We are selectively scaling our alternatives franchise while maintaining capital discipline, and our fundraising pipeline in private credit and infrastructure remains highly constructive.

Regulatory clarity on bank capital requirements has improved our visibility into capital return capacity. We repurchased two point eight billion of common stock in Q1 and expect to maintain an elevated pace of buybacks through the year, subject to the macro environment.

Consumer credit quality remains broadly healthy. Delinquency rates in our card portfolio are stabilizing after the normalization cycle, and we see no evidence of broad-based consumer distress despite pockets of softness in lower-income cohorts.

Net interest income came in at the high end of our guided range, benefiting from balance sheet optimization and a favorable deposit mix shift. We expect NII to remain resilient through the remainder of the year even as the Fed continues its measured easing path.

We are deploying AI at scale across the firm, from fraud detection models that have reduced false positives by forty percent to large language model copilots that are accelerating our software development velocity. Our technology spend this year will exceed seventeen billion dollars.

The probability of a recession has risen but it is not our base case. Tariff-related uncertainty is the primary risk factor, and we are stress-testing our portfolios against a range of trade escalation scenarios. The consumer is still spending, but sentiment has softened meaningfully.

Our commercial and investment bank generated record markets revenue, with equities up twenty-one percent year-over-year. Client engagement across derivatives, prime brokerage, and securitized products was exceptionally strong as investors repositioned around the new tariff landscape.

The mortgage origination market showed early signs of recovery as rates stabilized in the mid-sixes. Refinancing volumes are still well below historical norms, but purchase activity has picked up in key markets and our pipeline entering Q2 is the strongest in five quarters.

We reduced our efficiency ratio to fifty-nine percent this quarter, ahead of schedule on our multi-year target. Headcount optimization and branch consolidation remain key levers, and we expect further improvement through the back half of the year as technology investments begin to yield operating savings.

Commercial real estate remains the area we are watching most closely. Our office exposure is manageable and has been proactively marked, but we do expect some incremental reserve builds in Q2 as select metropolitan markets continue to adjust valuations downward.

We continue to operate under the asset cap, and our ongoing engagement with regulators gives us confidence that we are making the right investments in risk infrastructure. Lifting the cap remains a top priority, and we believe we are closer to resolution than at any prior point.

Our international franchise delivered double-digit revenue growth, with particular strength in Asia and the Middle East. Cross-border transaction volumes accelerated as multinational corporates diversified supply chains away from concentrated sourcing, which plays directly to our global network advantages.

We completed the exit of nine consumer markets during the quarter, bringing total divestitures since the strategy refresh to fourteen. The simplification is freeing up capital and management bandwidth, and we are on track to achieve our medium-term return on tangible common equity target of eleven to twelve percent.

Trade finance revenues were up sharply as clients restructured supply chains in response to the new tariff regime. However, we are cautious about the potential for retaliatory measures that could dampen overall trade volumes in the second half of the year.

Regulatory and compliance transformation remains a critical workstream. We are investing heavily in data governance and risk controls, and while these investments depress near-term returns, they are essential to achieving sustainable operating leverage as the organization simplifies.

Consumer spending through our card and digital platforms grew seven percent year-over-year in Q1. Travel and dining categories remain robust, while discretionary retail has moderated somewhat. Importantly, payment rates are holding steady, which tells us consumers are managing their balance sheets prudently.

We now have over forty-eight million active digital users, up eleven percent year-over-year, and Erica interactions surpassed two point five billion cumulative. Our AI-enhanced digital assistant is driving measurably higher client engagement and lower servicing costs per household.

The forward curve implies further rate reductions, which will pressure NII modestly in the near term, but our asset sensitivity has been substantially reduced through hedging. We are well positioned for a range of rate scenarios and do not expect NII to be a significant headwind in 2026.

Small business lending activity accelerated in Q1, particularly in healthcare and professional services. Our streamlined digital origination platform is reducing time-to-close by thirty percent and we are gaining share in the under one million dollar loan segment.

Wealth management delivered record net new assets of sixty-three billion dollars in Q1, driven by strong advisor-led flows and continued momentum in our workplace channel. Client assets surpassed seven point two trillion, and our pre-tax margin expanded to twenty-nine percent.

The IPO backlog is substantial and diversified across sectors. We led several marquee offerings in Q1 and the pipeline for Q2 and Q3 is robust, particularly in technology and healthcare. That said, window sensitivity is elevated given tariff-driven volatility, and some issuers are adopting a wait-and-see posture.

AI infrastructure is generating the largest wave of strategic advisory mandates we have seen since the cloud transformation cycle. We are advising on hyperscaler partnerships, semiconductor joint ventures, and energy procurement deals that are critical to scaling AI compute capacity globally.

Fixed income underwriting had a standout quarter as corporate issuers took advantage of relatively tight credit spreads to refinance and extend maturities. We expect sustained issuance volumes given the wall of debt maturing over the next two years.

Demand for our three-nanometer and advanced packaging technologies continues to significantly exceed available capacity, driven almost entirely by AI accelerator and high-performance computing applications. We expect AI-related revenue to more than double year-over-year in 2026.

Our 2026 capital expenditure plan of thirty-eight to forty-two billion dollars reflects the unprecedented demand environment. The vast majority is directed toward leading-edge nodes and advanced packaging, particularly CoWoS capacity, which remains the primary bottleneck for AI chip deployment.

Construction of our Arizona fab is progressing on schedule, with volume production expected to begin in late 2026. The facility will serve both commercial and government customers, and we have received strong interest from US-based design partners seeking geographic supply chain diversification.

The evolving US export control framework continues to create complexity for certain customer engagements. While our direct revenue impact from restricted shipments has been limited to low single-digit percentages, the broader geopolitical environment requires us to maintain flexibility in our capacity allocation planning.

Gross margin expanded to fifty-nine percent in Q1, reflecting favorable product mix toward leading-edge nodes and disciplined pricing on advanced packaging services. We expect margins to remain in the fifty-seven to fifty-nine percent range for the full year.

We generated over one hundred billion dollars of total net inflows in Q1, led by iShares ETF flows that benefited from continued rotation into fixed income and a broadening of equity allocations beyond US mega-cap technology. Our market share in ETFs reached thirty-four percent globally.

Our alternatives platform crossed four hundred billion in assets under management, driven by strong fundraising in infrastructure and private credit. Institutional allocators are structurally increasing their exposure to private markets, and our acquisition of GIP has positioned us as a top-tier infrastructure manager.

Aladdin's AI-enhanced capabilities are resonating with clients as portfolio complexity increases. We are embedding large language models into risk analytics and portfolio construction workflows, enabling portfolio managers to interrogate multi-asset exposures in natural language and generate scenario analyses in seconds rather than hours.

Tariff uncertainty is creating both risk and opportunity for active managers. We are seeing renewed client interest in systematic strategies that can dynamically hedge trade policy risk, and our multi-factor models have adapted quickly to incorporate tariff exposure as a priced factor.
